sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=19, col=30), MOUSE(row=25, col=42).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 51, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The puzzle involves green crosses on a grid. Clicking a cross highlights it and toggles a target cell (dark gray corners). Clicking the toggled cell then "moves" the cross to that position, consuming adjacent crosses. Currently 4 green crosses remain (crosses #1 and #2 were consumed). The goal is likely to eliminate all green crosses or move them to target positions.
- Plan: Click on each cross to identify which cell it toggles, then figure out the goal (likely placing crosses at specific target positions).
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 50 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Very strange! The current frame matches state 2 exactly (same green crosses at same positions), but clicking (19,18) which previously went from state 2 to state 0 now stays in state 2 with no changes.
This is really puzzling. The board state is identical to state 2, but the actions that worked before no longer work.
Wait - let me check the last_action_result more carefully. Maybe the action didn't actually execute, or maybe there's some other issue.
Actually, I notice something: the "Executed actions" still shows "MOUSE(row=19, col=30), MOUSE(row=25, col=42)" which are from a PREVIOUS sequence. This suggests that the action I just called might not have executed properly.
Let me check the last_action_result to see what happened.
Also, I notice the step went from 50 to 51, but the board didn't change. This suggests the action was a no-op or only affected the HUD.
Wait, but I called `action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 18}])` and the output says "After (19,18): state=2, Green: 5, Gray: 0". So the action did execute (step incremented), but the board didn't change.
This is very strange. Previously, clicking (19,18) from state 2 created a gray cross target. Now it does nothing.
Could it be that the game has some kind of cooldown or state that prevents repeated actions? Or maybe the game has changed in some way I'm not detecting?
Let me check the last_action_result to see if there's any useful information.
Actually, let me step back and think about this differently. Maybe the issue is that I've been using the wrong coordinates. Let me look at the actual pixel positions of the green crosses more carefully.
The green cross at (18,17) has boundary corners. Let me figure out the exact extent of this cross.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check last_action_result
print("Last action result:", last_action_result)

# Let me look at the exact boundary of the green cross at (18,17)
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in b)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in b)
        if (min_r, min_c) == (18, 17):
            print(f"\nGreen cross at (18,17):")
            print(f"  boundary: {b}")
            print(f"  pixels: {n['pixels']}")
            # A 4x4 cross has 12 pixels (4x4 = 16 minus 4 corners = 12)
            # The cross shape is:
            # . X X .
            # X X X X
            # X X X X
            # . X X .
            # Center is at (19.5, 19.5) for a cross starting at (18,17)
            # So clicking (19, 18) or (19, 19) or (20, 18) or (20, 19) should hit the cross
            break

# Let me check the ASCII representation around (18,17)
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nASCII around (18,17):")
for r in range(max(0,16), min(len(lines),23)):
    print(f"  row {r}: {lines[r][15:22]}")

# Try clicking at different positions on the cross
# The cross spans rows 18-21, cols 17-20
# Let me try clicking at (19, 19) which is the center
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 19}])
print(f"\nAfter (19,19): state={state_graph.current}")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
green = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
gray = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'g']
print(f"Green: {len(green)}, Gray: {len(gray)}")
print(f"Last action result: {last_action_result}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
